response:
"Sec. 1  
[The given equation involves a fourth-degree polynomial and an absolute value function. To find the number of real solutions, we can consider the behavior of the function on different intervals. Since the absolute value function splits the domain into two parts, we analyze the function for positive and negative values of x separately.]

Sec. 2  
[For x ≥ 0, the equation becomes (1/3)x^4 + 5x = 7. This is a continuous function that increases as x increases, so there is at most one solution in this interval. For x < 0, the equation becomes (1/3)x^4 - 5x = 7. Again, this is a continuous function that increases as x decreases (since -5x becomes larger as x becomes more negative), so there is at most one solution in this interval. Combining both intervals, the equation has exactly two real solutions.]"
